project_overview.project_development_objective: To improve the capacity of targeted communities and authorities to benefit from and effectively manage selected fisheries
project_overview.total_financing: US$55 million (IDA Grant: US$50 million + PROBLUE MDTF: US$5 million)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Estimated 165,000 direct beneficiaries in 12 targeted coastal communities, fishing households, MSMEs in fisheries value chains, staff from national and state fisheries authorities (FGS and 5 FMS ministries plus BRA)

primary_locations: Jubbaland (Calanley and Kibora sites in Kismayo)
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Yes – Somalia is classified as a fragile state with active conflict. Project covers all five Federal Member States and Mogadishu. Terrorist groups (Al-Shabaab) operate in Mogadishu and southern Somalia. Somaliland excluded at this stage.

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Increasing the Capacity of Dependent Communities to Benefit from Sustainable Marine Fisheries
key_components.description: Establishes or improves climate-resilient small-scale fisheries infrastructure at minimum 12 sites (cold stores, ice plants, jetties, markets, processing facilities) and improves fishing, handling, processing, and marketing techniques through value chain improvement plans, capacity building, safety-at-sea training, and support to cooperatives/MSMEs.

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Strengthening Marine Fisheries Governance and Management
key_components.description: Supports fisheries governance through legal/regulatory framework development, FMDC operationalization, climate-informed fisheries management plans, co-management pilots in 12 communities, MCS system development (VMS, AIS, patrol vessels, FMC upgrade), fisheries statistics and stock assessments, and strategic blue economy studies.

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Project Management, Monitoring and Evaluation
key_components.description: Supports PMU establishment within MFBE, procurement and financial management systems, M&E framework, communication plan, grievance redress mechanism, ESF compliance, coordination with other ministries and private sector, and knowledge sharing.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Fisheries and Blue Economy (MFBE) of the Federal Government of Somalia (FGS), with Project Implementation Units (PIUs) in each of the five Federal Member State (FMS) Ministries of Fisheries and the Banadir Regional Administration (BRA)
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee (Director General Technical Working Group – DG-TWG) chaired by DG of MFBE, meeting twice yearly; Fisheries Management and Development Council (FMDC) meeting quarterly as high-level policy platform; coordination with FAO, EU, USAID, bilateral donors through Fisheries Development Working Group
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (July 2016, revised September 2023); STEP system for procurement tracking; PMU-centralized procurement with no FM/procurement at state level; PPSD and 18-month Procurement Plan prepared; risk rated High (residual Substantial)
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: PMU-led M&E with dedicated M&E specialist; semi-annual World Bank implementation support missions; mid-term review; Results Framework with PDO and intermediate indicators; field surveys; third-party monitoring and GEMS tool for hard-to-reach/insecure areas; Kobo toolbox for data collection; stock assessments twice during implementation

identified_risks.risk_description: Division of roles between FGS and FMS not fully anchored in legal frameworks; FGS ability to lead and coordinate FMS participation uncertain; political pressure may interfere with activities; politically connected individuals influencing IUU fishing licensing
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Support FMDC as platform for policy dialogue; document criteria for investment prioritization; Inter-Governmental Fiscal Federalism Forum and Financial Governance Committee provide oversight; project can be stopped if licenses issued in contravention of federal fisheries law

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak capacity to respond to external shocks; climate shocks (drought, flooding) lower fish production and damage coastal infrastructure; insecurity in Mogadishu and south-central Somalia constrains economic activity; persistent humanitarian crises
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project fully financed by grants (no counterpart funding risk); support disaster risk management and revenue generation through fisheries to reduce medium-term macroeconomic risks

identified_risks.risk_description: Fisheries Master Plan (2023) recently adopted and needs implementation; new Fisheries Law needs harmonization between FGS and FMS; IUU fishing (including authorized/licensed vessels) influenced by politically connected individuals; weak capacity and low resources for fisheries management
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Support FMDC for dialogue and coordination; promote transparency; convene stakeholder dialogues; investment/activities can be stopped if FMS/FGS issue licenses in contravention of federal law

identified_risks.risk_description: Tension between sustainability of fishing operations and poverty reduction goals; complementarity of components requires both to function; risk of increasing fishing effort rather than improving value
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Focus on increasing value and reducing post-harvest losses rather than increasing catch volume; parallel complementary components; early concrete results to manage beneficiary expectations

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Capacity at all implementation levels still emerging; unclear roles and responsibilities between FGS and FMS; MFBE is first-time World Bank implementing agency with low administrative capacity
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Capacity assessment to identify building activities; learning-by-doing approach; engagement of non-government actors and private sector; qualified service providers to supplement government capacity

identified_risks.risk_description: Challenging FM and procurement environment; potential mismanagement, fraud, lack of transparency and corruption; weak and fragmented FM and procurement frameworks; inadequate institutional and human capacities; MFBE has no prior World Bank project experience
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: FM centralized in MFBE; use of COAPM and SFMIS; EAFS support from Accountant General's Office; direct payment to contractors/consultants; supervision engineer for construction; GEMS for remote monitoring; strict fiduciary controls; World Bank FM supervision

identified_risks.risk_description: High social risks including community tensions over fishing rights and infrastructure access; substantial GBV/SEA/SH risks; security threats from Al-Shabaab in coastal areas; land acquisition and resettlement questions; weak MFBE capacity for ESF management; risks of backlash from people leaving conflict/piracy-related livelihoods
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: ESMF, SEP, SEAH Prevention and Response Action Plan, LMP, Security Management Framework, RPF prepared; grievance mechanism established; gender-responsive design; site-specific ESIAs and ESMPs; capacity building on ESF; disbursement conditioned on ESF instruments clearance

identified_risks.risk_description: Large and diverse stakeholder array including federal/state officials, fishing communities, universities, private sector, international partners; tensions and frictions between groups can evolve quickly
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Comprehensive Stakeholder Engagement Plan with identification, analysis, engagement plans, communication activities and grievance redress; early FMS engagement; multiple communication channels

safeguards_and_social.environmental_category: High (Environmental risk: Substantial; Social risk: High)
triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS 3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS 5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement
ESS 6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes – project-level grievance mechanism required as disbursement condition; World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Accountability Mechanism (Inspection Panel and Dispute Resolution Service) also available
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Substantial gender focus: target 30% women beneficiaries; 20% of MSMEs owned/led by women; 8 co-management arrangements with at least 10% women participation and at least one woman in executive position; gender-responsive infrastructure design; training at convenient times/locations; women's leadership mentoring; aligned with World Bank Gender Strategy 2024-2030, Women in Maritime Strategy, and Regional Gender Action Plan for ESA; SEA/SH Prevention and Response Action Plan prepared
vulnerable_groups: Women (facing GBV/SEA/SH risks, limited access to resources and decision-making)
Youth (15-24 years old, limited sector participation)
Internally displaced persons (IDPs, including GBV/SEAH survivors working in fisheries)
Small-scale fishing communities in food-insecure coastal areas
People engaged in conflict/piracy-related activities transitioning to fisheries livelihoods

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly flagged as Fragile State and Conflict operation. Security Management Framework (SMF) prepared to address Al-Shabaab and other security threats. Site-specific security management plans included in ESIAs/ESMPs. Third-party monitoring and GEMS used for inaccessible/insecure locations. Project designed to provide alternative livelihoods to people engaged in conflict/piracy activities, with awareness of backlash risks. International firms encouraged to form joint ventures with local firms due to insecurity. Project contributes to WBG FCV Strategy 2020-2025 pillars 1 (prevention) and 3 (transition out of fragility). Piracy resurgence risk noted (late 2023/early 2024 incidents) linked to illegal fishing and lack of coastal community economic opportunities.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Over two decades of civil unrest; FGS and FMS face political contestation on constitutional matters and resource distribution; limited FGS/FMS presence outside urban areas; terrorist attacks (Al-Shabaab) mostly in Mogadishu and southern Somalia; one of world's poorest countries (GDP per capita US$592 in 2022); chronic food insecurity; persistent humanitarian crises; climate vulnerability (drought, flooding); domestic revenue insufficient for security and development needs; weak governance and transparency; IUU fishing influenced by politically connected individuals
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Al-Shabaab terrorist threat in coastal areas and Mogadishu; security risks widespread across Somalia; market instability due to country security risk limits international contractor interest; Security Management Framework formulated; collaboration with maritime police (trained by UNODC and EU EUCAP Somalia) on at-sea patrolling; all patrols led by fisheries authorities; GEMS and remote monitoring for hard-to-reach areas; security risk factored into procurement risk assessment
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes – partial. Internally displaced persons (IDPs) are present in fishing communities; approximately three-quarters of GBV/SEAH survivors are IDPs, some working in fisheries sector. Climate-induced displacement noted as risk (drought, flooding, storm damage to coastal infrastructure). Project does not directly target refugees but IDPs are among vulnerable beneficiaries. Resettlement Policy Framework prepared for potential land acquisition/involuntary resettlement from infrastructure construction.
